era_context: Late 14th century: Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China; Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans; Western Europe experiences the Great Schism; the Timurid Renaissance develops in Central Asia; the Joseon Dynasty rules Korea; the Aztec Empire continues expansion in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major chronicle centers

events.title: Peasants' Revolt in England (Aftermath and Suppression)
events.description: In the aftermath of the Peasants' Revolt of 1381, King Richard II and his advisors continued to suppress the rebellion and consolidate royal authority. The year 1382 saw the final suppression of remaining rebel forces and the execution of remaining leaders. The revolt had challenged feudal authority and raised questions about social hierarchy, though the immediate outcome was a reassertion of royal and noble power. The long-term impact on English society and labor relations was significant.
key_figures: John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, royal advisor)

sources.name: Knighton's Chronicle (Henry Knighton, contemporary English chronicler)

events.certainty_note: The suppression of the Peasants' Revolt in 1382 is well-documented in contemporary English chronicles. The date and events are confirmed by multiple independent sources.

events.title: Ottoman Conquest of Nish (alternative dating)
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murat I may have besieged and captured the Serbian fortress city of Niš in 1382 (though 1383 is also commonly cited). The capture of Niš was a significant step in Ottoman consolidation of control over the Serbian lands. The city became an important Ottoman administrative and military center. This conquest was part of the broader Ottoman expansion in the Balkans.
events.certainty_note: The date of Ottoman conquest of Niš is uncertain, with sources varying between 1382 and 1383. Modern historians are divided on the exact year.

events.title: Earthquake in China (Shaanxi Province, alternative dating)
events.description: A significant earthquake may have struck Shaanxi Province in 1382 (though 1383 is also cited in some sources). The earthquake caused considerable damage and loss of life. Such natural disasters were often interpreted as signs of heaven's displeasure with the ruling dynasty, though the Ming Dynasty's legitimacy was not seriously threatened.
events.certainty_note: The date of the Shaanxi earthquake is uncertain, with sources varying between 1382 and 1383. Precise dating of seismic events in this period is difficult.

events.title: Timur's Consolidation in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) continued to consolidate his power in Central Asia, establishing Samarkand as his capital and center of the Timurid Renaissance. In 1382, he was engaged in campaigns to secure his control over the Silk Road trade routes and to establish his authority over rival Central Asian powers. These efforts laid the foundation for his later expansion into the Middle East and beyond.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (biography of Timur, written early 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power in Central Asia in 1382 is documented in later biographical sources. The specific events of 1382 are not always clearly distinguished from surrounding years.

events.title: Ming Dynasty Administrative Reforms
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Hongwu Emperor continued administrative reforms to consolidate central authority and eliminate remaining Yuan Dynasty remnants. In 1382, the Ming government was engaged in reorganizing provincial administration, establishing the examination system for civil service, and strengthening the imperial bureaucracy. These reforms were crucial to the stability and longevity of the Ming Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Ming administrative reforms in 1382 are documented in official records. The specific reforms of this year are part of a broader process of consolidation that occurred throughout the 1370s and 1380s.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ottoman conquests in the Balkans (Niš) is uncertain, with sources varying between 1382 and 1383. The Shaanxi earthquake is also uncertain in dating. The specific administrative reforms of 1382 in the Ming Dynasty are not always clearly distinguished from those of surrounding years in surviving records. No major disconfirming evidence undermines the significance of the events listed, but dating precision is limited.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1382 is moderate but regionally uneven. European sources (English, Ottoman) are relatively abundant. Chinese sources are rich but sometimes lack precise dating for individual years. Middle Eastern sources are fragmentary. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania are essentially undocumented. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Ottoman and Chinese sources may reflect court biases.

graph_edges.note: The suppression of the Peasants' Revolt in 1382 was a direct consequence of the revolt of 1381.
Ottoman conquest of Niš may have occurred in 1382 or 1383; sources are uncertain.
Timur's consolidation in Central Asia in 1382 led to his expansion into Persia in 1383.
